title: FINS3666 Assessment Guide 2025 T1
body:
ASSESSMENT GUIDE FINS3666 Trading and Market Making Term 1, 2025  Assessment Summary Assessment Task Weighting Due Date* Learning Outcomes Assessment 1: Individual Assignment 30% Part 1 Week 3 by 9pm Friday CLO 1, CLO2, CLO4. This assessment is divided into three parts. Each part will be Part 2 Week 5 by 9pm Friday started by students as an in-class activity. Part 3 Week 7 by 9pm Friday Assessment 2: Group Project 30% Week 10 by 9pm Friday CLO1, CLO2, CLO3, CLO4. This assessment involves working in small teams to apply quantitative analysis and modelling techniques learned in the course to develop and evaluate trading strategies. Assessment 3: Final Exam 40% Exam period CLO1, CLO2, CLO3, CLO4. This assessment consists of 20 short answer questions that cover content from Weeks 5 to 10 of the course. * Due dates are set at Australian Eastern Standard/Daylight Time (AEST/AEDT). If you are located in a different time-zone, you can use the time and date converter.

Course Learning Outcomes (CLO) CLO1: Students will demonstrate a solid understanding of quantitative analysis in financial markets, including the different trading strategies employed and the importance of risk management. [PLO 1, 2, 3, 5] CLO2: Students will be able to apply statistical analysis and modelling techniques to derive insights from financial data, enabling them to develop and evaluate trading strategies. [PLO 1, 2, 4, 6] CLO3: Students will develop practical skills in back testing and performance evaluation, including the ability to design, conduct, and interpret back tests to assess the performance of trading strategies. They will also learn how to evaluate and optimise trading strategies based on the results obtained. [PLO 1, 2, 3, 7] CLO4: Students will develop critical thinking skills in assessing market microstructure, analysing order book dynamics, and understanding the impact of market liquidity on trading strategies. [PLO 1, 2, 6]

UNSW Business School 1  Assessment Details Turnitin Turnitin is a tool for checking originality and preventing plagiarism. It compares submitted work against other students' submissions, the Internet, and key resources. If required to submit via Turnitin, a link will be available in your Moodle course site. You can submit drafts before the deadline and use the Similarity Report to refine your writing before the final submission. You can find out more information in the Turnitin information site for students.

Late Submissions The parameters for late submissions are outlined in the UNSW Assessment Implementation Procedure. For FINS3666, if you submit your assessments after the due date, you will incur penalties for late submission unless you have Special Consideration (see below). Late submission is 5% per day (including weekends), calculated from the marks allocated to that assessment (not your grade). Assessments will not be accepted more than 5 days late.

Extensions You are expected to manage your time to meet assessment due dates. If you do require an extension to your assessment, please make a request as early as possible before the due date via the special consideration portal on myUNSW (My Student profile > Special Consideration). You can find more information on Special Consideration and the application process below. Lecturers and tutors do not have the ability to grant extensions.

Special Consideration Special consideration is the process for assessing the impact of short-term events beyond your control (exceptional circumstances), on your performance in a specific assessment task.

What are circumstances beyond my control? These are exceptional circumstances or situations that may: • Prevent you from completing a course requirement, • Keep you from attending an assessment, • Stop you from submitting an assessment, • Significantly affect your assessment performance.

Available here is a list of circumstances that may be beyond your control. This is only a list of examples, and your exact circumstances may not be listed. You can find more detail and the application form on the Special Consideration site, or in the UNSW Special Consideration Application and Assessment Information for Students.

Generative AI and Translation Tools

You may use AI-based tools to research and prepare before completing your assessments. Standard wordprocessing features such as spelling, grammar checking, and reference generation are allowed. However, functions that generate, paraphrase, or translate text, including for assessment tasks or presentations, are prohibited. Generative AI can assist in researching topics but is prone to inaccuracies and fabrications. Always verify the information it provides with original sources and consider diverse perspectives. Avoid sharing personal or copyrighted content on platforms like ChatGPT, as it operates through OpenAI. There are resources available about how best to use generative AI available here Chat GPT & Generative AI at UNSW.

When you have used AI, you should ensure that it is noted, such as in the online workbook in Assessments 2 and 3. As the other written assessments are personal reflections, using Generative AI as part of your research is not necessary and, as stated above, cannot be used in generating, paraphrasing, or translating texts. For more information about AI and assessment, see here Using AI in Assessments.

UNSW Business School 2  Please note that your submission will be passed through an AI-generated text detection tool. If your marker has concerns that your answer contains passages of AI-generated text, you may be asked to explain your work. If you are unable to satisfactorily demonstrate your understanding of your submission you may be referred to UNSW Conduct & Integrity Office for investigation for academic misconduct and possible penalties.

Acceptable use in background research: • Idea Generation: Use generative AI to brainstorm potential topics or angles for your assessment. • Preliminary understanding: Consult generative AI to get a basic understanding of a topic before diving into primary source research. • Research assistance: Employ generative AI to provide overviews of complex topics, which you will then verify with primary sources (e.g. Scite) • Diverse perspectives: Use generative AI to identify various perspectives on a topic, ensuring to cross-check for accuracy with reliable sources.

Unacceptable use in producing an assessment task: • Content creation: Do not use generative AI to write or draft any parts of your assessment submission. • Paraphrasing: Avoid using generative AI to reword or paraphrase passages for inclusion in your work. • Translation: Do not use generative AI to translate text that will be used in your submission. • Direct editing: Do not use generative AI for editing functions beyond basic spelling and grammar checks provided by standard word processing software. • Reference generation: Do not use generative AI to generate citation formats for inclusion in your submission.

Documentation and integrity: • Disclosure: Clearly note any use of generative AI in your research process, as required, such as in the online workbook. • Verification: After using generative AI for research, thoroughly verify the information against original sources and ensure understanding of the material. • Personal reflections: Do not use generative AI at all for personal reflection tasks, as these are based on your own thoughts and experiences.

In case of review: • Explanation preparedness: Be prepared to explain your work and demonstrate a thorough understanding of the content and sources of your submission if questioned. • Academic integrity: Maintain the integrity of your work by avoiding the use of generative AI for tasks where it is prohibited and follow all guidelines for proper citation and originality.

Following these guidelines will help ensure that generative AI is used as a tool for enhancing your research and learning, rather than replacing your own efforts and understanding in the creation of academic work

Assessment 1 In-class Activities Due date A1 Part 1: Friday Week 3 by 9:00 pm A1 Part 2: Friday Week 5 by 9:00 pm A1 Part 3: Friday Week 7 by 9:00 pm Weighting 30% (each activity is worth 10% of the 30% overall mark) Task Written report, code/spreadsheet Word limit 500 words Submission Via Moodle course site Note: If using Turnitin include whether similarity report will be made available to students

Learning Outcomes Assessed This assessment addresses the following Course Learning Outcomes (CLOs): • Build foundational skills in quantitative analysis, focusing on trading strategies and risk assessment. • Apply statistical and modelling techniques to analyse financial data and inform trading decisions. • Develop practical expertise in back-testing and evaluating trading strategies for performance optimisation • Strengthen critical thinking by analysing market microstructure, liquidity, and order book dynamics.

Description of assessment task Assessment 1 focuses on building your foundation in quantitative analysis, including statistical techniques, algorithmic trading, and market microstructure. Through practical activities like order book analysis, trading simulations, and backtesting, you’ll connect theory to real-world applications. Weekly discussions and case studies will help you critically evaluate trading strategies, preparing you to apply these skills in upcoming tasks and professional contexts.

Approach to the assessment task Attendance to lectures is crucial for successfully completing A1, as each activity builds the foundational knowledge and practical skills required for quantitative trading. Lectures provide not only theoretical insights but also hands-on practice, and discussions will deepen your understanding of statistical techniques, algorithmic trading and market microstructure. By participating in the in-class activities, you will gain the critical thinking and practical application skills necessary to tackle real-world trading problems, ensuring you are fully prepared to excel in the next assessments and beyond.

Activity 1: Statistics & Microstructure Analysis (Week 3) • Students pre-process and clean a dataset of order & trade information for stocks and ETFs • Students calculate standard microstructure measures based on order book and trade feed data • Students will use statistical techniques to determine relationships between stock prices and microstructure measures or other dependent variables

Activity 2: Pricing & Spread Models (Week 5) • Students will conduct data processing, statistical & microstructure analysis on a new data set • Students will implement pricing & spread models calibrated to the new data set

UNSW Business School 4  Activity 3: Trading Strategies (Week 7) • Students will conduct data processing, statistical & microstructure analysis and implement pricing & spread models on a new data set • Students will design and implement a trading strategy for a Proprietary Trading firm • Students will design and implement a trading strategy to act as Market Maker in an ETF.

Submission instructions Detailed instructions, datasets, and additional resources for this assessment are available on Moodle. Please ensure you download the necessary files and review all guidelines before starting your analysis. If you have any questions, refer to the course forum or contact your instructor.

Activity Title Due date Activity 1 Statistics & Microstructure Analysis Friday Week 3 by 9:00 pm Activity 2 Pricing & Spread Models Friday Week 5 by 9:00 pm Activity 3 Trading Strategies Friday Week 7 by 9:00 pm

Submit the following via Moodle: • Cleaned Dataset: Include the cleaned version of the dataset you analysed. • Analysis Report: Submit a 500-word report addressing the assessment questions • Code or Spreadsheet (as applicable): Provide the code or spreadsheet used for your analysis.

Supporting resources and links Please check Moodle for Supporting resources and links for the assessment.

Assessment 2: Group Report Trading Strategy Development, Backtesting & Evaluation Due date Week 10 Weighting 30% Task Written response Word limit 2,500 words maximum Submission Via Moodle course site Note: Similarity report will be made available to students

Learning Outcomes Assessed This assessment addresses the following Course Learning Outcomes (CLOs): • Demonstrate an in-depth understanding of the principles, practices, and complexities of financial technology and innovation within global financial systems. • Critically analyse and evaluate real-world financial problems using technological solutions and datadriven decision-making frameworks. • Collaborate effectively within diverse teams to deliver well-structured and professional group outputs that reflect critical thinking and strategic planning.

Overview This group project assessment provides you with the opportunity to apply quantitative analysis, statistical modelling and risk management techniques in the development and evaluation of trading strategies. The project encourages hands-on experience with data processing, backtesting and financial performance analysis, equipping you with essential skills for professional trading environments. You will work collaboratively to analyse financial data, implement trading strategies, conduct backtests and refine your models based on real-world constraints. Emphasis is placed on critical thinking, market microstructure understanding, and risk evaluation, ensuring alignment with the Course Learning Outcomes (CLOs) and Program Learning Outcomes (PLOs).

Description of Assessment Task

You will work in small teams to apply quantitative and modelling techniques learned throughout the course to develop and evaluate a trading strategy. The assessment involves:

• Designing and testing a trading strategy using historical market data. • Critically evaluating the performance of the strategy through backtesting. • Proposing improvements to optimise the strategy. • Demonstrating initiative by incorporating advanced techniques beyond the standard course material.

As a team, you will complete a well-structured report, incorporating statistical & microstructure analysis, backtesting, performance & risk measurement results and a critical reflection on strategy performance. High-quality submissions will exhibit a well-reasoned strategy, strong quantitative analysis, and a depth of understanding that extends beyond standard methodologies.

Approach to the Assessment Task

Instructions for Students – Preparation Instructions

UNSW Business School 6   • Team Formation: Students must form teams and submit their group details via Moodle. If no selection is made, automatic allocation will occur by the end of Week 3. You don't need to notify the Course Convenor about your choice of groups unless you have a non-standard request. • Preparation: Review lecture materials and readings related to: • Statistics & Microstructure Analysis (Weeks 1 & 2) • Market Microstructure Models (Weeks 3 & 4) • Market Quality & Know Your Alpha (Week 5) • Backtesting & Performance Evaluation (Week 8) • Risk Management & Optimisation (Week 9) • Dataset Selection: Choose one of the historical market datasets available on Moodle.

Assessment Guidelines & Questions Step 1: Data Processing, Microstructure Analysis, Pricing & Spread Models • Use a tool of your choice (e.g., Excel, Python, R) to clean the data, if needed • Calculate statistical & microstructure measures (as per Activity 1) as if you were receiving the data in real time • Implement appropriate pricing and spread models (as per Activity 2)

Step 2: Select either a Proprietary Trading or Market Making strategy (e.g. Activity 3 options, although not exclusively) • Provide a clear statement of your trading strategy and include all key assumptions necessary for implementation • Please also include an explanation on why you selected the specific trading strategy

Step 3: Backtesting • Formulate a trading trading plan for your strategy, i.e. entry & exit signals, position sizing • Perform a backtest of your trading strategy using an appropriate historical data

Step 4: Trading Strategy Implementation Extension • Propose an extension to your trading strategy and include an explanation of your choice of extension • Examples of extensions to the trading strategy implementation include: o Improvements in Price Prediction model, e.g. use of AI / ML techniques, advanced statistical techniques, addition of event flags, sentiment analysis o Improvements in Risk Management, e.g. advanced inventory management, adverse selection models, volatility indicators, advanced liquidity measures o More realistic modelling, e.g. transaction costs, short selling restrictions

Step 5: Repeat Backtest • Repeat the backtest with the revised trading strategy that incorporates any extensions to the original trading plan

Step 6: Performance & Risk Measurement • Provide analysis of your backtest with respect to a set of o Performance metrics o Risk metrics o Risk / Return metrics o Other metrics if appropriate • Compare the results between your initial and subsequent backtest.  Step 7: Critical Reflection • What are the strengths and limitations of your trading strategy? Please use the analysis conducted to support your arguments • Provide your top three (3) recommendations to improve the implementation of trading strategy in a real world setting and explain your reasoning.

The table below shows how each of the three activities within Assessment 1 are related to Assessment 2.

Step / Task Proprietary Trading Market Making Data Processing A1: Activity 1 A1: Activity 1 Microstructure Analysis A1: Activity 1 A1: Activity 1 Statistical Analysis A1: Activity 1 A1: Activity 1 Basic Pricing Model A1: Activity 2 A1: Activity 2 Basic Spreads Model A1: Activity 2 A1: Activity 2 Basic Trading Strategy A1: Activity 3 A1: Activity 3 Trading Strategy Extension A2 A2 Backtesting A2 A2 Performance Measurement A2 A2 Risk Measurement A2 A2

Required Deliverables:

1. Group Project Report – Maximum 2,500 words detailing methodology, analysis, and findings. 2. Cleaned Dataset – The processed version of the dataset used for analysis. 3. Code or Spreadsheet – The computational scripts (Python, R, Excel) used for the analysis and backtesting. 4. Peer Evaluation Form – Each team member will submit an evaluation of group contributions.

File Format: PDF for the report; CSV for datasets; Python/R/Excel files for code.

AI Transparency Requirement: If AI tools (e.g., ChatGPT, ML models) are used, students must document their usage and provide justification.

Supporting Resources & Links Please check Moodle for resources.

Assessment 3: Final Exam Weighting 40% Task Examination Period (Week 11) Word limit 20 Short Answer Questions (2 Marks Each) Submission Via Moodle course site Note: Similarity report will be made available to students

Learning Outcomes Assessed

This final examination assesses students on their ability to:

1. Apply financial technology and algorithmic trading principles to real-world scenarios. 2. Critically evaluate trading strategies using quantitative and statistical modelling. 3. Demonstrate an understanding of risk management and market microstructure concepts. 4. Communicate complex financial insights effectively through structured responses.

Description of assessment task The final assessment is a comprehensive examination designed to test the knowledge, application, and critical thinking skills developed throughout the course. It includes problem-solving questions that require you to analyse financial data, evaluate market dynamics, and assess trading strategies. The exam focuses on applying quantitative techniques, conducting backtesting, and demonstrating strategic decision-making in a trading context.

Approach to the assessment task

The final examination will cover key concepts discussed in the lectures from Week 5 until the end of term. More specifically, the following table outlines the content that will be examined:

Week Content Questions Marks 5 Market Quality 2 4 Know Your Alpha 2 4 7 The Role of Technology 4 8 8 Backtesting &Performance Evaluation 2 4 Strategy Robustness 2 4 9 Risk Management & Optimisation 2 4 Strategy Refinement & Implementation 2 4 10 Advanced Topics 4 8 Total 20 40

Exam Instructions

• You will answer 20 randomly selected short-answer questions, each worth 2 marks. • Your responses should be concise, well-structured, and supported by reasoning. • Time Limit: 2 hours. • Submission: Responses must be submitted on Moodle before the deadline. • Academic Integrity: All work must be your own. Plagiarism or the use of unauthorised assistance is strictly prohibited.

Assessment Guidelines & Security Measures

To ensure fairness, security, and integrity, the following measures will apply: • Randomised Question Pool: Each student will receive a set of 20 questions from a larger question bank. • Timed Responses: The exam will be strictly time-limited to 2 hours. • Turnitin Not Required: Given the technical nature of responses, plagiarism detection tools such as Turnitin will not be used. • AI & External Tools Restriction: The use basic AI text editing tools is permitted only to support clarity of text. Use of AI must be acknowledged. • Moodle Accessibility Compliance: The exam is designed to be fully accessible for students requiring accommodations (e.g., extra time, screen-reader compatibility).

Marking Criteria & Partial Credit Guidelines

Each question is graded out of 2 marks, with the following marking structure:

• 2 marks – Complete, well-reasoned and clearly structured answer with use of supporting evidence or examples when necessary. • 1 mark – Partial response with some errors or missing key justifications. • 0 marks – Incorrect or missing answer.
